risks: APIM Consumption tier does not support rate-limit-by-key policy; only subscription-level rate-limit is available, limiting true per-user throttling granularity
No managed identity configured on App Service — OpenAI and Cosmos DB connections would require key-based authentication or manual identity setup
Azure OpenAI has disableLocalAuth set to false, leaving API key authentication enabled against WAF security recommendations
Public network access is enabled on both Azure OpenAI and Cosmos DB, increasing attack surface without private endpoint isolation
Cosmos DB serverless is single-region only with no geo-redundancy or automatic failover capability
B1 App Service plan lacks autoscale — may bottleneck under sustained or bursty AI workload traffic
APIM Consumption tier provides no SLA and introduces cold-start latency (~1s) on first request
correctionsRecommended: Add a system-assigned managed identity to the App Service and assign Cognitive Services OpenAI User and Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor RBAC roles for keyless authentication
Upgrade APIM to Developer or Standard tier to enable rate-limit-by-key for true per-user rate limiting, or document the per-subscription-key-per-user approach as the rate limiting strategy
Set disableLocalAuth to true on the Azure OpenAI account and use managed identity with RBAC for all data plane access
Add an Azure Key Vault resource for centralized secret management (connection strings, API keys during transition)
Consider adding private endpoints for Azure OpenAI and Cosmos DB to restrict network access and align with WAF Security pillar recommendations
Evaluate upgrading App Service plan to S1 (autoscale, deployment slots) or P1v3 (VNet integration) as traffic grows
